speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with George Nichols, Frank Herzog, and Glenn Gilder took place at George Nichols' home in Harvard on May 28, 1975. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you guys remember Joe Wells? Who?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Joe Wells.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You mean up at Deary?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. Yeah, I knew him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Joe and Chuck and Roy. Roy, I think. Roy was a sheep herder. I knew him. Herd and G.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Frank knows a lot more about country night.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I left her. In 1917 I went right by their place and over the hill and worked at Camp 7 out of Deary. On Brush Creek.

George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder

words: Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I hear he was a man of few words.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He never said what. Great big guy. He was strong as a bull. He only had one eye.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That big Gill?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: The biggest coward he ever saw. He was a little bit of a guy. He was right all the way.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, he had a cat just above the flat creek bridge up there in that flat too. When I was here in the shop, I used to go out with George with horses. When we'd take a horse out, I'd been crippled and take him out and bring others back. I had an old coyote. He had a warned -kneaked hand and a buggy. I used to go out and help him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You mean now a Deary back towards the ..., don't you?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No. No, we went back in there on the flat about as near as I can remember, about halfway between the hand place and the bridge. I never was in there but once. We took some horses in there for him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I never was in camp back in some place.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: The big gill was a coward even though he was a big man. I thought he was just a real tough guy from, you know, him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, there I was working on the helmer. They had a highline too with bringing in logs.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You see them camps were put in after I left here. See when they took the headquarters away from here, they took it to Bobal. Well, that's when they went to donkey engines. They sold off half of their horse, you know, picked out the poorest ones and sold them because I helped take them to Potlatch too. They started number seven and number eight up there. Well, number eight was donkey camp and number seven was horse camp. And I went up there and I worked a couple of months I guess in camp seven. And then I come down here for the Fourth of July celebration. That is in 1970. Well, then I left here, you see, and I didn't come back for eight years.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And this one camp over here was the first one. Well, that and seven was the only ones that I worked in. See, I come from this camp over here and into the harness shop and worked here in the shop until they closed that.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: How many camps did they have in this area? How'd one in their back of Bennett's? What? How many camps did they have in this area?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They had one back of Bennett's there someplace. That's the one you were, you carried lunch to. No, I carried from this up here one. Right over the hill there, the Thompson place, down on the river, followed the skidding trail out. It had one horse and a sled.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, that was the one over on that place of Ben Stewart's, where that spring is over there on the flat, on the old Poon Jill place, was there a camp there?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, there was a little tie camp.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: What the hell was his name now?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's what I was going to say, there were some little tie camps around.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Was it Lutz, sir?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, Jimmy Lutz, he'll run the camp where you used to live.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I can't speak of it now.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: What camp did they log your own creek from? Four?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, they...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: What camp logged your own creek?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think that most of the state up here in this camp helped Maple Creek. They had a road right over the hill, just a little hump between the two of them. I think most of them stayed there. They used to have a road, you know, run up to our place and right up that ridge, here too, the camp.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And then they had the branch up Maple Creek here, too. I was working for Campfield and helped me with the scatter of the ties across there, across the flat.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And that camp up where you folks used to live, there must have been some sign of that there when you come here. Oh, it was all there.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Arms, bunkhouse, cookhouse. You know, there's one up there on, well, the town maker's place. Yeah, that's where they put you.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And this one up here.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I don't know whether that...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, it must have been about all of them in this area. That and on the Malcolm Place, that was closed up. I know the first year I was here, there was a fellow that had a watchman in there. But I don't think it ever run after that.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It started up again, did it?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I know a couple of the David boys, well, Pete and Henry and Fred Little and I, we was out hunting. And we didn't take no lunch with us. Didn't get back in the figure. We hit the camp over there. I don't remember who was watching, was standing there watching the camp. And we get him up for some lunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time period is this that we're talking about with the camps that you're running down here? About what year would this be? By 1906 to 1908, wouldn't it, George?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Five to six. I left here in seven, so I don't want to think about that. Well, what about the... You came here when the... They were first... When the railroad was going, and that was in 1904.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you happen to come here?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, on railroad work, I was too darn small to get much than the else would do. I lived over north of Spokane over Deer Park. So I come over and I got on in clues, leading carts there, and I'm rough cut just out of clues. When I got them finished, I come on up to Princeton. See, they had the great camp down there on the Cheney Place. You come in across Prince John's and then across there, you know, the camp was there on that flat. See, we changed that river channel right there with tribe rhymes. You used to come in there, you know, water backs up in there yet. Comes in through there. The river used to make that curve in there. And we changed the channel there to do away with that and just put that cover in there. Just go to bridges. Then we come from... From there, we come up here to... Right over here on the Canfield Place.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It was right there on the flat below the hill, just beside the cut. There was a river. It come over on the other side. There was just a little stream come around through there. Then they had the camp as the cook tents and that stuff. They had them out on the flat. Then on this side of the creek, they had the stables and the skinner's tents in there. The surveyors had the tents there and Jim Hamm was cooking for them.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Did you hear...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was the company advertising for men to come in and work?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you find out about the work here?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, I... No one of the fellas was over here or to him. I was just a kid then. I don't know. 16, 17.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember what it was like when you first came here? Was there a lot of activity and hustling and bustling around?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, this town wasn't here then. It wasn't started until afterwards. That's the days when you went with your bed on your back. Things a lot different in our day. But potlatch was just being built when you first came here then. They were just putting up the frame. The mill, when I come from the woods and come up through... I walked from the close -up branch of my bed on the back. The bed was bigger than I was. I don't know. I don't know. I thought I weighed more. 75, 80 pounds. I never did weigh 100 pounds. I was 22. The whole wheel of scrapers and this dump, I couldn't look over the top of it. Do you remember...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you here when the mill first came in when it was built? What? Were you here when the mill was built?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. He was born here. No, he was two years old when it was built. Two years old. I thought you were born here. I did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about that? What happened in the area?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Damn, I don't know. I mean, a lot of new people coming in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they put the mill up fast? Was it an overnight or did it take a long time or what?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: If I remember, it didn't take too long. No, they put it up pretty fast.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And it came in pretty quiet. They just came in and did their job. Well, what they done, you know, that was a story anyhow. There was a fellow come in, you see the old man Peterson, that had the place where a barter used to live, you know. He owned that land over there and there was potholes all over it. About all that it was good for was pasture. Of course, there wasn't much pasture then. They didn't have the cattle. And this fellow come in and he just looked like an average man, you know. And he looked the land over and went and talked to Peterson, told him he was looking for a place to start a mink farm, I think it was. And he said that looked like it would be a good place for him, and kind of those sloughs in there, you know, the water. And old man Peterson told him, well, it wasn't much good for anything else. And if somebody could make some use of it, well, I guess they'd just well have it. And I guess he sold it to him pretty cheap. And just a short time after that, the other come in and start surveying for the mill. No, that darn place down there, you take all around that mill all through there, like when they put in water pipes and these old slabs and all that stuff, that's the way they fill them up, and then dirt over the top of them. The whole darn thing was a mess of sloughs.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And yeah, well, the old fellow here the other night from Tico, I can't remember his name, well, the names don't mean much to me, but he used to live out on deep railroad main line there and then come back. Well, it didn't go over quite sure. It showed in the picture just where the river run. Oh yeah, I can remember that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They drove logs there before the mill came in, right?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh yeah. Well, hell, the blues. They bought out another outfit, you see. This other outfit had three mills. They had one loose and one at Elton. I guess the other one was at Colfax wood in the front. That's right.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And the pot lights had a dam in it up here. They drove logs down the river to that dam and unloaded them out here on the track.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, we used to drive on through before the cracks was... Yeah, of course. Well, they did a couple of years afterwards. The only loaders they had, see, when I left here was them on the flat cars, them, oh, what the deuce was they called them. It was on big skids, you know, they just slid them along on the flat cars. Had a donkey engine on them. Slide ass. Slide ass, that's it. The old slide ass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these, did they do much logging before pot lights came in here? Did Cod log very much in this country?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, they didn't some, but not too much in.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: But that's when they drove the logs down to Clues in the dark. They had a mill in Clues.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they had another one too, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Colfax the other one?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. I think it was Colfax. Yeah, on 1 Albertan.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Was that Cod?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think that was the name. It wasn't too much logging going on until the pot lights come in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what were these river drives like that they used to have? Was it a lot of lumber and a lot of, they'd take a lot of men?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Nothing like the, I mean it wasn't anything like the Clearwater drives, of course. Well, I'd imagine they're pretty good drives. See, they used to make that drive for all three of them mills when they made the drive. These flats up here, they used to be all underwater, just like a big lake.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think, well it was in 1970, wasn't it, that pot lights made their last drive?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think. You know, they had the headquarters here, they had a track at Hawaii and there were matches are now for Turner Agency. They had the little chaise engines for the sidelines for bringing the cars into the main line. That's when the little chaise engines down there in Lewiston, sitting there in that little park, only they'd take the side drives off of it. It don't look like they did. See, instead of the big engines and the piston they had, well they had, they was geared down, you know. They had pistons on the side and gears down there. Oh, I see.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, there's a burn ferret living up here at the schoolhouse when you was here.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Just across from where the schoolhouse was. Well, there was two cabins in there when I was here and the one fella, I don't remember what he's damn, and I can't think of the other fella's name now. Oh, Margaret Benson and I was talking about it. Oh, then in the hospital, he used to work, you see, David's logged up Maple Creek area. The old man David and Pat had a contract near the time the old man pulled out.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Travis or something like that was a guy's name.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I don't remember he and his wife with the ferrets. And she decided to see the old shea right by there and went out Maple Creek.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: She decided she was going to raise some chickens. So she sent three or four hands out there. That old shea going by, I said, she didn't have one of them that had.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Them old tracks was pretty rough in -house, so most of all that handwork, you know, just left them up the best of things. Well, a big engine wouldn't have stayed on the tracks.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Wonder if it is, isn't it?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It's a wheel.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Huh? It's a wheel?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: The moment the shea is coming, you can hear it. It's a wheeling for two miles. That's what they had to lie down there for, you see, to turn their engines around. They'd come in, headed down, and then they'd go in from this way and back out on the other spur. You see, and they was headed back again.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They have gotten one up at Bogle, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these towns, Harvard, Princeton, start up as soon as the railroads got in here, as soon as they got the track to the town?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, this started up pretty quick after that. Of course, it never made what they figured. They thought their mines were going to make a big thing at that time, and they figured they were going to run a railroad up there at the mines. Up the north fork of the river, but up the main branch of the flues. But the mines didn't turn out that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Harvard, did they think Harvard was going to be a real town?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, yeah, they figured it then, yeah. They figured it would be. It probably would have made a pretty good little town if the mines had turned out like it would be. Or they left a logging operation here instead of going on up by there. Of course, either the Gold Bug mine, which was rather than the Daisy mine, was running at that time.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's interesting. They came in about 1905, and the mining was still pretty healthy. It looked pretty good, the future of it. I guess the Gold Bug and the Daisy both was doing pretty good at that time. I know there was a couple of fellas working up there in this camp out of town here. They had mined quite a lot. They left the camp and went up the Gold Bug to work. One of them I talked to him after he went up there, and he said they were making it good up there. But I guess they lost the vein, finally. The vein run out.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I often wondered if they ever found anything to amount to anything in that Gold Bug.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He said they were doing good. He said they were getting out pretty good at that time. But whether they lost the vein or whether the vein run out, I don't know. As his name was there at last, he was just right on to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For now?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: For now. He was going to hit it right away. He was just a little ways from it, but he never did hit it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He never did have anything.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: How about the Mespots?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Did they ever ship out of there some ore, but it didn't amount to much. It didn't amount to much.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They did ship some ore out of there, though, didn't they?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: A little, I guess.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You mean the Mespots?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: One year they shipped a little bit.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they built up that loading dock over there. Then the Jews tried it, you know, they were doing the correction. They shipped that stuff, but they couldn't. It's rich in copper, but there isn't enough other metals with it to make it pay. That's what trouble was. See, the Mespot, they had a stamp bill and everything up there. There were Jews mining in there during the Depression. While the Jew outfitted in Spokane, they bought up all those old mines. That ore they shipped, that was taken out of the slag bag, you know, if it hauled out. Mespot, a young Mespot, he said if they could even get enough out of it for the shipping, they might get the things started and make it pay. I think he shipped two -car load, and they didn't even get enough out of it to pay for the shipping. Then this other outfit, they had the same luck. They couldn't, and they gave it up. I guess they still own the mines, as far as I know.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Marvin Darrow, do you know him?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, you knew his dad, Curly. Yeah. He's up in the Coeur d 'Alene, and he's buying all that stuff he can get, even yet. He is. He's got a lot of faith in him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Curly, he had a lot of faith in mining, too. I don't know. They just get the bug that way. Yeah, I think so. Well, you know, they sat on Gold Hill, or they drilled there for that steel. They claimed that was good, but the Coeur drilled, you know. You remember here, oh, just too many years ago, I think Art Kinman and several of them that had quite a little in that. And the same way of that up there by the men's ball. And above that was two brothers lived in here, and one of them had a family. And they was prospecting around. Well, they prospected all up in that country, and they claimed they had struck it rich up there. And so they tried to make a deal with the Kellogg outfit, and they come down and looked it over. Well, these brothers, they hadn't looked into the ownership of the land or anything, you know. It doesn't think about that and the mining outfit. Well, you probably remember that, because there was quite a lot in paper about it here. Well, that was going on, but they just went and got lease on the land. These guys that had struck it, well, they couldn't do anything. It didn't make out they were going to get a big dig, and they didn't get anything out.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they, who was it?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Somebody was telling me just a short time ago that they hunted all over that country up there. And they said there's a cord just all over up there where they, they brought big outfits in there. And cord drills, you know. And they gave it up, but then that don't mean anything. They might have it there and just leave it like that, and they're ready to work it. Could easily be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever know any of those mines that did hit it big? Any of them what? That any of the mines that really did hit it, that were yielded good gold? Well, they sure hit it up under who do's that gold?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That was a fighter mine. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were pretty good weren't they?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't those old guys do pretty good in there?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Some of them done pretty good up there. Oh, George Stephan's made money out. Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Of course they was plaster mining, most of them mining. That's where the money was made, was plaster mining. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I guess the Chinaman made it. Chinaman's, they got gold up there too.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: One thing a lot of them, I guess, didn't get out of the country with it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Killed them all. They've got a story of going that there was a bunch of chinks on, on cameras.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Or...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: There you know, and they have a story going around that some guy went in there and killed a whole bunch and took their gold. Six or seven of them.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think that's right. We've all heard that. Well, I was telling you from TECO here the other night. I know the name. He was telling me that some old -timer, whoever he got these pictures from, told him that they caught a fellow that killed some of the Chinaman. And he had a trial, they had a trial in that log cabin for him and hung him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Here, in this country?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, down there by the potlatch. Well, the cabin sat right there where the mill is.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I know that old man Chinaman up here, he used to hold eggs and pork and... Oh, a bunch of junk up there and sell it to them chinks. And Gus was just a little fella then. And they wanted Gus. And old man, he was going to give him to him. He had just a lot of scare guts today. But, yeah, before we come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did those Chinaman come in after the whites had taken the place of mining, gotten most of the stuff?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Those chinks, I think, was here when the first of them, along with the rest of them.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Now, that bunch up there, they were here before I was.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They had done a lot of digging up there. There was still lots of sign of where they worked that trick all over up there. Way back away from it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, that old man Pierce, you know, he used to be in there on the boulder creek. Then he had a cabin up there. Well, they said it just about on the line between Ernie Shauver and Rand's. You know, there used to be a spring back up there. There used to be a little creek run down that road all the time. The timber's taken off, of course. Well, there's quite a few of them cricks that dried up after timber's taken off. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Used to be one come down there off your place, you know, in that pocket there. That used to run a little year round.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they worked over every creek on this side of the river.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And tried to do anything on the other side.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, that figures. Big creek. No, strip night. Even more cup it, but I don't think they found too much on strip night. The place up there where they build a trench from one part of the creek, clearer around the hill to another.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: But I don't know if they found anything or not.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That was the funny part of it. The chink never told anybody what he found. He kept that quiet.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, the white man, when he struck it, he had to go and have a big hoorah out of it and let everybody know he'd made a strike.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, what was that, the waste, though, mine, Frank? Was that a gold mine or, you know, used to be up there near the fork of the flutes?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, the old man and his son were the plaster mining up there. It was gold up there.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they had quite a tunnel in there and had a machinery in there. During the war, they got the machinery out of that for scrap metal.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think all the gold they got, though, was out of the creek. When they worked in that dredge and they got down there, the old poor man, Creek Quark, come in. They done pretty good right there.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they played them better on up the river, but George wouldn't let them run up that way. Well, I'm glad he didn't. They sure spoiled that by the way they were then. Glad was sure a pretty place in there. Well, they got out just in time, too. You see, they passed that law that they had to put the land back like it was and put a channel through there for the river.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That must have been some of the most beautiful picnic area in anywhere that's in this country. Well, it used to be you could drive up that river this side of the North Fork, big cedars over the road there that you couldn't see the sun much times.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I don't know. I just can't get excited about going there and picnicking and swimming because I know with all the tailings it just isn't natural anymore. No, that was covered with pretty nice timber. The old road didn't go up the river like it does now. It was up on a ridge, up on top of the hill there. Yeah. It came back down the river there right below poor man.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I used to make me quite a stake up there. I would come down and get me and drive a greater team for him, like sometimes 30 bucks. Gee, that was big money for a kid. When I left the shop, I went up with the... I sent me up with the surveyors to help surveying out them proper kingdom mines. It was up on all over them hills. Boy, that was a job, cutting thread and stuff in there. Then when the surveyors got finished, they sent me out the morn down to here in the liver stable to get a team to get them out. I walked down the morn and went back in the afternoon with a team and walked out of Copper King.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Copper King, what was that? That was on up the ridge from the Mezpo?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, it was below the Mezpo, just before you started up the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear stories about the lost mines and money buried in the gold, buried in the hills?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I don't think... around here, right now. I never heard of it. I didn't either.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You can hear them stories most anywhere.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No,

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: if anybody got any money out of these mines by robbing it or working it or any other system, they spent it right away.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I heard that Adam Carrico was up in the mines for an awfully long time. Even after he lost his sight, he stayed up there for years. He did. Then they took him to the farm. I know he tried to get Roy Gurdsie to sign papers to get him out so he could go back up there. He was on the county in the end, just to get the groceries from Roy before they took him away.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: My doctor includes there... He had quite a story going there for quite a while on that lost wheelbarrow mine. It was just a promotion.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: How many finagling scheme?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's what it was. They were going to have a school in there, the regular town. They had that old wheelbarrow, and the old rifle they found where one guy had killed him. That was Johnny Browning's old rifle. He'd been out hunting. I guess it was an old army rifle. The thing backfired. He'd throw powder at his face, and I guess he'd have had it over his stoutens unless it had the rifle they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story on that? This doctor in Palouse was promoting the mine?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: The best place to get the dope on that is to go back to the old numbers of the Palouse Republic, or the daily Ithonia in Moscow. They could have got it all down in black and white. I remember when it was going on. I didn't believe in it, and none of it registered very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't believe it at the time? Did you?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, I didn't know anything about it. No, there was a lot of them putting a lot of money in that though. Oh, sure there were. I think old George Arndt put a lot in that. Could have. Yeah, could have. Oh, I guess he was convincing Tucker. Hell, no, he got a lot of money out of it. I don't know where they got the skeleton that they... It was a doctor, probably one that he got through with, and no one was digging up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the deal with being the lost wheelbarrow mine?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, that's the mine he was just talking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but I mean, why did he have to have a gun and a skeleton and a wheelbarrow?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they claimed that two fellas had this mine, and it was worth it, and that one of them killed the other and left him in the mine, and then he skipped the country. Well, it was old.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And this fella, what was it? This fella that was supposed to have skipped it, wasn't he supposed to have come back again?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He was old, and he confessed that he had killed the other fella and told them all about it. Seems to me that's the way it was. I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then this doctor discovered the mine?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they went up and found the mine, and found the skeleton in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had the wheelbarrow and the bank window down there, didn't they, Bob Batch?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: One of the old wooden wheelbars.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. Oh. Well, there was some of these old -timers in the early days, they were just as full of BS as they are today. Oh, come on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were as bad as today?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, there wasn't quite so many of them, man. They made it a little more realistic than what they had today.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they had the mine fizzles, and the logs pretty well played out, but by golly, they had a pretty good little agricultural area here. It turned out pretty good. For quite a few.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It didn't turn out very good so far this year, the lizard. No, it isn't. It's pretty wet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got your crop in?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. Lucky, just by the skin of my teeth. No garden to speak of. I don't think anybody's got garden around here, or the crops you mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you work in the woods around here? Around here?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. I didn't. Not right here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, whereabouts?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You were over working for Big Gil.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: What a big one. What a Deary and bogle and a hammer.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, you know, they seen that timber around bogle. Well, you don't see no more timber like that.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Three logs on the flat car.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: These didn't last too. Big white pine. There was nothing to get a bogle along white pine. Clear, 1 ,000 feet in it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Aren't they any brush in the country?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's right.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Hardly any limbs on them. It's thin, tall, and loose as thick. And the land didn't take them long to slaughter it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Funny, they just went through the country and took a face off of this and then pulled out and went up there. I wonder why.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Does it look better?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I wonder about how they... Well, of course, it's other outfitted logs around here. Quite a little. I don't know. It was big timber here. Well, they... I suppose they figured they had more timber up there. And I suppose later they got in here and sent them this timber to go down. Cut bigger too and log it. I don't know what the... I wasn't around here. I don't know what the deal was. Well, it just stopped. Like now, as soon as a piece of ground gets a tree or two on it, it'll make a toothpick or two while they're logging it again. Of course, what they'd done then, at that time, you see they couldn't log this steep land with the horses. Then after they got into the donkeys, why they could come back and log this steep land.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Up there they...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They used highline, didn't they, with the donkeys up there in eight?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Eight and fourteen. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: There was one down there. Big donkey ended. They brought it over there in the bottom of the cup. You see, they got them big donkeys first, like they used on the coast of that big timber. They was too big for this timber here. Most of it. Well, they'd hit a stump or something when they was dragging in. Oh, well, they'd... Well, Choker just cut it right in two and keep on going. But that Arcadia Archer company, when they was clearing that land over there, they thought one of them donkey engines would be great, you know, for clearing land. They'd come over here and bought one of them big donkey engines. And they unloaded... Below Deer Park, the little town we called Pratt then, isn't much there anymore. And started out there from the town with this donkey. And I don't think they got any farther than from here to where Becker lives up there now, with the thing. Of course, that's gravel, you know, rocky soil over there. And they just pulled their anchor stumps. They couldn't get nowhere. Their anchor stumps wouldn't hold them. And that thing was sitting there, at the last Ivernote, it sat out there for years and years.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, and then left them old dog keeps all around in the brush thing.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You go...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, down towards Marble Creek in there. There were three or four sitting around. And then Blackwell logged on top of the hub there. Oh, out from Fernwood. They logged clear around then and come down Crystal Creek, the shade. They got through on top. They let the shades down near the side into Micah Creek. Mute them down there the same way with the donkeys. And they got through on Micah Creek and left the whole shade and all.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, that's right. I've seen them. Used to go back in there hooker and lay it in the fish. That's probably one of their old donkeys that's in St. Mary's. Quite likely.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Of course, as he was growing in there now, they took... I think they cut those shades up for jokes. I think they did. The donkeys are going too. There's one down on...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Still one down on the Russell Creek. That's right.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It's on the little creek called Bear Creek right back to the pole.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, I got a picture of my wife standing on that like she was running it. But it's been robbed until there was nothing there but the boiler anymore.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think John East can tell you where all that stuff went. I don't know.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You aren't insinuating, are you?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for these first camps after the railroad was built in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for them? You had work with the cooking?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I worked out here in the first camp out of Harvard here. Oh, I worked there in the kitchen while plunking. I don't understand a work inside. So then I was a kraulhound. Took care of the stables.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell us last time we were here about bringing out the meals to the men while they were working in the woods?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, that's what I was talking about a while ago. That's when they had the lunch ground down on the... I said I had one of the pictures of it. They had that down on the river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the food be that the men would have there?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they didn't carry lunches then like they do now. See, they took out a hot meal to them all the time. They had these big 50 -pound lard cans that, well, lard and different stuff used to come in them. They had lids on them, you know. They took the meal right off the stove and put it in these cans, the different kinds of food in the different cans, see. And then in front of this sled, they had a cupboard built up there with the shelves in it. That was for the pies. There's always pies that they took out at noon. And they put the pies in there with these cans. They would take an old quilt called them soubins then and lay one of them down in the bottom of the sled and then put these cans on top of that and cover them up with... with some more of them quilts. And then the dishes was all tinware, you see. But to have them in the box, well, they built a table down there out of poles. And they had a place for campfire and a big kettle for that. There was a lot of French Canadians then in the camps. And of course, they all wanted tea. So there was tea and coffee both for breakfast and supper, but for noon, it was all tea. Well, I'd take the tea out dry in a can, too, and they'd send a man in from the woods about 11 o 'clock, and he'd start fire up there and put the water in this kettle. And when I... the time was when I'd get out there just a little ahead of time, you know. Well, I'd put the tinware all up on this table.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And then when the men come in, I'd throw the tea in the pot. They liked it strong. It'd float a wedge. And when they was all there and ready to eat, I'd just take these quilts off the top of the can and the lids off and put one of them big long spoons in each can, and everybody helped themselves.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I was trying to think, where the hell it was. I worked in the halls of Lonschaupt that way with the tea.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I can't remember where they lived with that.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They used to all down on the drive, too, you know. Yeah, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What drive?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's when they'd drive the logs on the river. Every driver, he had a little sack that he carried between his shoulders with a lunch in it. Then they'd have a certain place that they'd meet, works handy for the bunch of them to get there, you know, and start a fire there. It all out, me allowed it that way, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, on those drives, would they log during the winter and haul them down and deck them on the bank? Is that what they'd do?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they'd deck them out in the woods and slay haul them in the winter. Unless they was close enough to the river that they could skid them or dry them in as gold devils. Well, they had dollies, too, with wheels on two -wheeled dollies. They'd make the wheels out of wood, cut off a chunk off a log the size thickness through they wanted, and then put iron bands around it, or a hole through the center of the axle.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Where in the devil was it I read in some book where someone was telling about the whole log and then they're telling about those dollies, and they said they had iron wheels. That's all below me, that was wood with iron hopes around it.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, yeah, that's right. Yeah, well, I'll tell you, Frank, I was reading just lately about a wagon traveling across the area right here between Walla Walla and the Dow. And this writer was telling about having trouble with the brakes on that wagon. They were on the front wheels.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, when in hell did you see a brake on the front wheel on the wagon?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I don't know. You'd better be on the road here writing up the country that just been in there two or three years and they know it all about it. I hear, I guess just last year, just to give you an instance, there was a fella, he sat down one day and talked to him and he was telling me all about the Legion cabin down there.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Who built that? When it was built?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: The whole thing, and I never opened my mouth, I just sat there and let him talk. And when he got through, I said, it so happened that I worked on that when he was building. And it was something that when he told it, I could build it. And I said, that's the American Legion cabin. I said, if you look outside on the fireplace, you'll see the plate there. It'll give you the year that it's built and everything. And I told him, I said, I worked on that and worked on the all -through and it was built and things. Well, not all -through, but putting in the foundation and all that. That was them four fellas from St. Mary's that done the wallwork. Sitka's.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No, Sitka's didn't do it, didn't they do that?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: No. There were four big suites from St. Mary's. You just made a business of that. Them logs were all veed underneath. And then that heavy oak and the tar put in there, you know, and when you turn over on the round side, the more they settled, the tighter they get. The batch is the way it is with a lot of this stuff.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: So you'll hear something, you know?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Glenn, what was the name of that horse thief that, was that Plummer?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Plummer. Henry Plummer was the head of quite a gang. Yeah. Henry Plummer is told how to route through this country.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That old cabin at the griddle camp there, that old log cabin on the hill was supposed to belong to him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Is that right? Where was he then?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That old log cabin on the top of the hill there at the griddle camp.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, yeah. That's old.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, what's one guy's name?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That's Robinson. Or somebody else.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Now, Robinson was down this side.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, anyway, I don't remember any beyond that, the green griddle camp.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Which road? Old County Road?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, it was close to the old county road going to Hoodoos. The cabin was beyond the meadow, east of the camp, proper, you know, the old meadow camp there. I've heard that that was a Henry Plummer outfit, but I don't know.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: We do know that Henry Plummer made tips from...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He set up there, too. I don't know if he had a cabin or not. Yeah, Bill and John and Ed and Charlie lived up there in a cabin for years. Somebody jumped Bill's when he was out in the harvest. That's what he told me, and he never done anything about it. He just let it go, and that's when they cooked out that other place.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He just liked the canfields, you know. They used to live down there by the river, in the start, for the Bill Fyatt House up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that right?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Down here on the river. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And then...

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It was like old Albert Daly. He lived down there on the flat.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yes, that's right. Way down. Down there, the barn down there. Yeah, where the big barn used to be. There's a log cabin there. Well, the Canfield House was over here, right out there, from that cut when they put the railroad in. It was still standing there when they went through that cut to the railroad.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Some old apple tree that said, yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I've heard that Canfield is the one that named Harvard. He did. Well, this place up here. This was his land. He named it Maple Creek.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He didn't name it Harvard, did he?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, part of the farm.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I think he donated a lot.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He's a damn railroad, anyway. He donated ten acres to him. So I know we were scattering ties out there when Bill Derry and... They got T .P. Jones with him. And they just come off the flowage up there. They were looking for a place to build a depot. Well, they had figured, you know, I'm putting the mill up there, and then decided I have room.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And anyhow, they stopped there and they asked, Can't anybody take enough land over here to build a depot?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And he studied a little bit, and he said, Well, if you build a depot there, where I was, he said, I'll just deed you ten acres. Well, there's a lot of them. They all laughed at him. But he told me after they went home, he said,

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: If they'll put a depot in the warehouse there, he said, I figure that's the richest piece of land I've sold. He said before they wanted to go anywhere. They had to drive out, put the team in the barn, and pay on that, and they'd come back again no matter how long it was gone. And here he said, All I've got to do is walk down there at the depot and get on the train and go, he said. And the same when I come back. And he said, I can take my hay to the warehouse there, and grain and put it in the warehouse. And he said, I've got a place here of wood too, and I'll bring wood out. And he said, I don't think I could do any better than that. He couldn't. No, he couldn't. He couldn't.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Right through his door.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He just brought that mark at 25 miles towards his door, didn't it?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, I used to haul the hay to Blues, $5 a ton. The hog cord was to Blues. They got two or two and a half acres.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I can remember, a gentleman's up here during that depression, they hauled it to Blues for $2 .50 a ton. That's what I was saying.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I have to work with four horses for two days to make $2 .50 and $5 .00 for that. Well, he had to stay overnight down there. Oh, yeah, that cost us damn near $2 .50.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to Gary? Know that guy at all?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: He wasn't the guy that done much talking about it. He was pretty much all busy. He was a rough old cuss. One thing, though, if a man had a grief, he'd stick up for you. I had one thing about the old boy. But he was a guy that worked from the bottom up. He was just talking right ago. He'd scrap you in a minute, you sassy.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I never talked to old Gary. I've seen him many times. Well, there I does talk to him a lot of times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like there?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, I had to pan know you talked to him.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Old business.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, he thought about it. Oh, it looked to me like if he had pot lights and he's bringing them out. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Of course, they're done a lot more for the little stump rubbers up here than anybody they've had since. Oh, yeah. Larry said that anybody that had bought land from the potlatch was entitled to graze their stock up here over the potlatch land. Well, as soon as he was out, that guy that got in there next, while he started driving you see the lease that land out, he said, well, I didn't know the people and the thing, and why they should have free pasture. I don't know. I went to Larry once about cedar. When I couldn't get cedars, I needed for shakes. And I would see him buy it and he'd give me an order right away for the land department to give me an order for cedar.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, that's something that's getting scarce from now.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Shake timber.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I've been putting that all into the fences. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I always thought it was such a shame that the tour cedar grove out. That was one of the prettiest places in the country. The birthed sold it to the cedar savage and that ended the cedar grove in a hurry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that at?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: It was at a drum creek.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: A Meadow creek.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Or a Meadow creek, I should say. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, you could walk up that creek before they cut any of the timber. Maybe it was just dark in cedar and white pine. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: That big creek was the same way. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Old Bronco and Elog been there on big creek. A lot of them great big white pine. They fell a little bit dory while some trees never took a log out of. For sure a lot of good timber wasted there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sometimes I get the feeling that if Potlatch had been more careful or had thought ahead more when they were cutting, they'd still have a lot more timber left nowadays than they do. Well. Seems like they'll have a hard time finding timber today. If they'd have done different, there'd have been different lot of ways. If they'd have seeded in the timber like other companies before them did on their skid roads and all that stuff, they wouldn't have had all the wash.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I know up there north of my place, you remember that road used to be in there north of me?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. You could drive clear back up in there. And there during the war and they went back and logged that. It was used in pans, you know. And that was all industrial. They seeded that fall range gun that was a grown up. Yes, it would have. speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, there was a jam. The thing come around made a curve in there. Instead of being so that it would throw the logs out in the current to go down, they'd throw them out again the current. And they jammed in there. Of course, they signaled back to hold the logs. And they was breaking that jam out. This fella, he was standing on logs here. They could get a log loose while they'd pry it down, you know. The peavey didn't get started. And he was standing up on a log head. The other one just pryed away. Away. The guy on top, while he got in a hurry, didn't wait for his signal and he started the logs down because they didn't see him coming. And they hit all these blinds, you know, and they hit that and him like... Instead of just throwing up off logs and they come right down and they put between them two logs and just smashed a foot to a pulp. So when Moore lost his arm about the same way up there on that slide, he went, you know, that was... As old Frank Featherstone called that, he was hooking on the jammer. And Frank signaled before Sylvan had his hook set, where it should be. He just caught the edge of seeing and raised the logs. And of course, I guess, throwed Sylvan off his balance and the log come down right on his arm. And old Frank, he had no business giving any signals at all. That wasn't his business. No. He was an important son of a gun. Yeah. Well, he's like old Josh Hillmer when Josh was in and that well, he was wearing a warehouse in Bogle. I was in there after about a day. He said he was the only man in the potlatch had that would work. He said he was Jipp on that warehouse in Bogle. And, yeah, he didn't do a day's work in a month. Right. You see, Frank was under T .P. Jones. Yeah. He come in the shop there one day and they had to, well, they sent a bunch of ties down the river and they jammed over there on the glass. And he come in the shop there and told me to go over and break that jam.

words: They didn't even have a tub and a washboard for you to do your wash with. You wasn't where you could get it done by somebody. No place to take a bath. If you wanted a bath, you got a bucket of water and stripped off in the bunkhouse and took a sponge bath. Yeah.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder

words: If you wanted men, you was walking in. The tow team would come along and they could pick you up and give you a ride in. If you quit or got canned, you were walking out. If the tow driver picked you up, he'd get canned.

words: Six days a week. Lots of us used to work on Sunday so that we didn't have to pay the board for a dead day. You know, that board went on the same Sunday and all while you were working or not. Yeah, if you were off a day while your board was charged, it's the same.

They got a man to go in there and take timber claims. And then when they proved up on the timber claims, well, I suppose they staked him while he was on the claims. speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: A lot of them was homesteads, and then some of them bought... Like that old man, Kinman, up there, he owned half of the river up there one day. Then that person come along and he'd borrow money on a lot of it, and he lost the whole shebang, except money. He put it there on the river.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, there was a bunch of big creeks. See, Fred Kinman, he was up a big creek. Oh, Wall Eaters, they was up a big creek. And the place I had up there, those two brothers, was homesteaded up there, and then they... When they proved up, well, one of them had the Hornby place out here, and he lost that door of depression there in 93. The way I knowed that was a well up there on the other place, other part of the old cabin was left there. And Bill Sexton, he'd been telling the whole time what a good well that was. Well, along the summer, it went dry, so I was going to go down a little deeper in it and see if I could get water while I was working in that, and there was a couple of fellas come up and draw. And one of them told me, he said, Well, you should... or asked me what I was doing, and I told him that well, it had gone dry, and I was going down deeper. And he said, Won't do you any good. And he told me that that was their old home, that his father at home said that, and that his brother at home said it less than their. And he said they dug that well, and they used to use that while it lasted, and then they hauled their water up from Boulder Creek. Boulder Creek and Lost Creek was in there together. That's why I think they... You know, that's right across from where Humison lived. He left that draw. Well, there used to be the two cricks run down there, you know, the year round, two little cricks run together, and then we dried up after we took the timber out of there. And that old Pierce I spoke of, he had a cabin right there in the forks there. Oh, well, that was there, and we logged in there during the war. And they burned it out of there. Burned it out of there, I guess the dold's it out.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. The higher guards put them out, armed guards in all the camps. Well, they, we had the A .F. Bell, you know, and the A .F. Bell and the CIA, they had both signed pledges that they wouldn't strike during the war. And they wouldn't run in on the other union.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder

words: Us young fellas, we'd take a team and go out there and get logs and make four or five hundred dollars a month. That was big money then. Sure was. We put ten swampers out of a job. You put all of them out of a job.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: They were right, too, when you come right down to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you say then the wages got cut down?

words: Yes, they did. They fixed the gyppo next. That's right. Well, they used to take over 600 men to run that mill down there. Now they say less than a hundred runs.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yes, sir. I've seen ten of them old guys. That was big yellow pine, you know, on Brush Creek.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Big stuff.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Ten of them old guys out there are swamping. And the skinner, he had a chainer. He didn't even hook his tongs or his chain. He just drove the teeth. That was the chain in there then. That was three men every team used to be. Colder than hell I would have. Deep, 32, 35, 0. I had to build a fire to keep them free.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Down here when there was gyppo and I know my brother and I don't know whether him or Abel Johnson was skidding together now. Anyhow, there was two outfits that were skidding together and Yark Cochran, he was swamped for the two of them.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, well, you know, in the big yellow pine logs, like was on Mount Margaret or where you were talking about, a man didn't actually, didn't need a swamper. He could kill a team and do his own swamping.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: And big limbs, they had the, they didn't have no chainsaws then, they had to use an axe saw. Shocked them big limbs off, but you know that's right.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: You could give a team all the damn logs he wanted in a day and then big logs, because there would be two or three of them that wouldn't have a limb on them. And like all your time, you got them out there. Team had up a good sweat. And he needed a limb or two.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, I'm an old guy that said these lemons, those big limbs, and you'd hear them. And that axe wouldn't be hit hard enough to hurt a fly.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: So a lot of those guys were just putting in their time and getting their pay. They were past their, they were, they were the lemons. They did all they were supposed to.

words: Oh,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were first here and you saw the logging they did the first few years after the Potlatch came in, what was the kind of logging that they were doing, the way they were going about logging?

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Well, instead of power saws, it was all done with these crosscut saws, the hand. They were cut into the log lengths right into the woods, all mostly from 12 to 16 foot, some up to 20's, about as long as they cut. If they would back away from the river, why they decked them. At that time, put them up in the high decks, you know. Then in the winter, why they'd slay all into the river and deck along the river bank. They're out on the ice, then when the ice broke in the spring. But they'd have these dams shut, the ones that were above, these dams. So they'd hold the water back up there, and as soon as the ice was breaking up, then they'd break these roadways out and drive them down the river.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder

words: You see, in the early days, when they were logging with horses, why they didn't hurt the ground or the timber like they do now. They didn't. And everything in the country comes with it. Just tearing everything all up, building roads, and they wash. It's rotten, they're just ruining the damn country. speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: I guess that's right. One hundred percent. They did up here, up the river, there during the war when they were working there in the woods. You know, they were hollering about conserving timber for future generations. Well, at that time, they didn't have blades on the skid cats like they've got now. I don't know, they had two or three of them big cats, you know, down the dosing. Well, they'd go up around and make the roads out on these ridges. And then these other cats do the skidding. Well, God, they'd plow right down through there, nice white vines up there, a lot of them twenty feet high and higher, you know. And they'd just plow right down through there, everything went down. Well, instead of skidding maybe a quarter of a mile to that place, they'd probably a hundred yards or so in their way, they'd just plow right down through again. They didn't try to save any timber they sell, but then they wanted the little fella to. Yeah. Well, yeah. Paul Lachas Tree Farm, we've seen that picture for years, but we've seen damn little of it here in the woods. We've seen it in the magazines, but we didn't see it in the woods. Well, you know, they had that paper they used to put out during the war there. They talked about how they was doing with their logging up in the Clearwater country, that they were marking the trees, just certain trees that they was cutting, and how they was seeding the roads afterwards so they'd go back to grass. Well, I talked to a lot of fellas that worked up there and said they wasn't doing any different up there, and they did here. But I was working up in there with my hunting business, and it wasn't any different up there than it was right here. They went in and took all those trees that were marked, and that was a hundred percent of them.

speaker: George Nichols or Frank Herzog or Glen Gilder
words: Goddamn forestry nowadays, they don't amount to two cents. They'd go right in and do, now like up on Big Creek, there was the nicest stand of second gross white pine, about like that.

words: And a lot of it, too. They'd go right in there and let granite cut every goddamn bit of it. And they'd talk about conserving timber, and go in there and ruin the second gross stand of timber that they was in the country.

words: Yeah, and these clean burns that they'd talk about, that brush grows up and that holds the snow.

words: That timber is what used to hold the snow, and they didn't have any floods like they have since.

words: Kim Blaine. Back in them times, you had politics a couple of months before election. And then when the election was over, politics was over a couple of months before the next election.
